title: Agentic RAG with Contextual AI (managed platform: query reformulation, parsing, instruction-following reranking, grounded generation, LMUnit evaluation)
one_liner: Deploy a managed end-to-end agentic RAG pipeline that autonomously reformulates queries (expansion, decomposition, multi-turn) and combines a vision-based document parser, an instruction-following reranker, a groundedness-optimized LLM, and natural-language unit testing.
problem_solved: Traditional RAG takes user queries as-is and glues together independently tuned components, so ambiguous, under-specified, or multi-part questions retrieve poorly; complex documents (tables, charts, multi-page financial reports) parse badly; conflicting sources get ranked arbitrarily; generators hallucinate beyond retrieved evidence; and there is no systematic way to test response quality before production. This notebook shows a managed agentic pipeline (Contextual AI) that handles all of these as one orchestrated system: the agent autonomously decides whether a query needs expansion, decomposition into sub-queries, or multi-turn context before retrieval, and each downstream stage (parser, reranker, grounded LM, evaluator) is purpose-built for enterprise RAG.
failure_modes: Short or ambiguous questions return poor retrievals because the query lacks context the corpus needs
Complex multi-faceted questions that require reasoning across several unrelated documents fail with single-shot retrieval (need decomposition into sub-queries)
Answers ignore conversation history in multi-turn dialogues
PDFs with complex tables, charts, and figures are parsed incorrectly, so critical numbers never make it into the index
Chunks lose their place in a long document's section hierarchy, breaking cross-section reasoning over hundred-page reports
Knowledge base contains conflicting versions of the truth (old vs new docs, internal vs external sources) and the retriever can't be told which to trust
Generated answers are ungrounded or hallucinated, mixing parametric knowledge with retrieved evidence
Model answers confidently even when retrieved context is irrelevant instead of declining
No repeatable, fine-grained way to evaluate RAG response quality (accuracy, evidence support, relevance, causation vs correlation) before shipping
when_to_use: Enterprise or financial document analysis where PDFs contain dense tables, charts, and multi-page quarterly reports (the demo uses NVIDIA revenue slides)
User queries range from simple lookups to multi-hop quantitative comparisons across several documents, and you don't want to hand-route them
Your knowledge base has conflicting information and you can express resolution policy in plain English (e.g. 'prefer internal sales docs, weight recent docs higher')
Groundedness and attribution are compliance-critical: you need the model to answer only from sources or explicitly decline
You want a production-ready pipeline in hours via SDK/GUI rather than assembling and tuning parser, chunker, retriever, reranker, and generator yourself
You need a built-in evaluation harness (natural-language unit tests scored 1-5) to validate and regression-test the system
Sending documents to a managed cloud service is acceptable for your data governance
tradeoffs: Vendor lock-in: the whole pipeline (datastore, parser, reranker, GLM, LMUnit) is Contextual AI's proprietary managed service, accessed via API key; you cannot swap or inspect internals
Documents must be uploaded to a third-party cloud datastore — unsuitable for air-gapped, on-prem, or strict data-residency environments
Ongoing per-query API cost and a paid subscription after the 30-day trial; not a free/open-source technique
Agentic query reformulation and multi-stage processing add latency versus a single vector lookup
Limited transparency and tunability of chunking/indexing/retrieval compared to building with LangChain/LlamaIndex; debugging is via platform UI, not code
Reranker model shown is English-focused (ctxl-rerank-en-v1-instruct); multilingual corpora need verification
Overkill for simple, single-document, low-stakes Q&A where a basic retriever + LLM suffices
The notebook demonstrates a product rather than teaching a reimplementable algorithm — the 'agentic' reformulation logic itself is a black box
how_it_works: The notebook builds a complete RAG agent on Contextual AI's managed platform, then dissects its four core components. (1) Setup: initialize the ContextualAI SDK client with an API key, create a datastore (client.datastores.create), and ingest financial PDFs (client.datastores.documents.ingest); the platform automatically parses, chunks, and indexes them (ElasticSearch vector DB under the hood). (2) Agent creation: client.agents.create wires the datastore to an agent with a grounding-focused system prompt, agent_configs (multi-turn toggled off for determinism), and suggested queries. At query time (client.agents.query.create) the agent autonomously analyzes the incoming query and applies a reformulation strategy — multi-turn contextualization, query expansion, or decomposition into sub-queries — before retrieval; retrieved evidence is inspectable down to page images via query.retrieval_info. (3) Component deep dives, each callable standalone: the /parse REST API parses PDFs with VLM+OCR, concise figure captions, and enable_document_hierarchy so chunks carry section-structure metadata; client.rerank.create with model ctxl-rerank-en-v1-instruct reranks documents following natural-language instructions plus per-document metadata (source, date) to resolve conflicts; the /generate endpoint (Grounded Language Model) answers strictly from a supplied knowledge list, with an avoid_commentary flag and refusal on irrelevant context; client.lmunit.create scores query-response pairs against natural-language unit tests (1-5 scale) across dimensions like accuracy, evidence, causation-vs-correlation, and relevance, visualized with pandas/matplotlib polar plots. Although orchestration is the dominant stage, the technique spans indexing (managed parsing/chunking), query-transformation (agentic reformulation), post-retrieval (instruction-following reranking), generation (GLM), and evaluation (LMUnit).
key_dependencies: contextual-client (ContextualAI Python SDK) — requires Contextual AI account and API key
requests (direct REST calls to api.contextual.ai /parse and /generate)
pandas, numpy, matplotlib (evaluation aggregation and polar plots)
key_code_signals: from contextual import ContextualAI; client = ContextualAI(api_key=API_KEY)
related_techniques: query-transformations: the agent's autonomous reformulation (expansion, decomposition, multi-turn) is an agentic, automated version of manual query transformation
reranking: the instruction-following reranker refines standard reranking by adding natural-language conflict-resolution policies and metadata awareness
self_rag: alternative open agentic architecture that adaptively decides when to retrieve and critiques its own output
crag: alternative corrective architecture that evaluates retrieval quality and falls back to web search
adaptive_retrieval: alternative that classifies queries and picks per-category retrieval strategies (this platform does it autonomously)
multi_model_rag_with_captioning: related approach to the parser's VLM/OCR handling of tables, charts, and figures
hierarchical_indices: enable_document_hierarchy pursues the same goal — preserving document structure for retrieval over long documents
reliable_rag: shares the groundedness/attribution goal that the GLM bakes into the generator itself
evaluation_deep_eval: alternative evaluation framework; LMUnit is the platform-native natural-language unit-testing counterpart
grouse: alternative grounded-QA evaluation approach comparable to LMUnit
summary: Agentic RAG (demonstrated on Contextual AI's managed platform) addresses the failure of traditional RAG on ambiguous, under-specified, or multi-part queries: an agent autonomously analyzes each incoming query and applies the right reformulation strategy — multi-turn contextualization, query expansion, or decomposition into sub-queries — before retrieval, with no user routing logic. The notebook builds a financial-document agent end-to-end: create a datastore, ingest NVIDIA revenue PDFs (auto-parsed, chunked, indexed), create an agent with a grounding system prompt, and query it. It then exercises four production components standalone: a VLM/OCR document parser with document-hierarchy metadata for complex tables, charts, and multi-page reports; an instruction-following reranker (ctxl-rerank-en-v1-instruct) that resolves conflicting sources via plain-English policies like 'prefer recent internal docs'; a Grounded Language Model that answers only from supplied knowledge and declines when context is irrelevant, minimizing hallucination; and LMUnit, which scores responses against natural-language unit tests (accuracy, evidence, causation-vs-correlation, relevance) on a 1-5 scale. Costs: vendor lock-in, cloud document upload, per-query API fees, and black-box internals versus open frameworks.
notes_for_chunking: 76 cells: 46 markdown, 30 code — unusually markdown-heavy and well-narrated. Structure is clean and hierarchical: an end-to-end build under numbered '## Step 1-5' headers (auth, datastore, ingestion, agent creation, querying), then '# RAG Components Deep Dive' with four self-contained '## N. Component' sections (Parser, Reranker, GLM, LMUnit) — each is an independent mini-tutorial with its own concept markdown, code demo, and 'for more examples' link; chunk at these ## boundaries. Junk to strip: cell 0 is a book advertisement, the final cell is a tracking-pixel image, one %pip install cell, dotenv/API-key boilerplate, GUI-walkthrough cells ('Optional: inspect in the UI') with remote screenshots, and heavy utm-tagged marketing links throughout markdown. Code outputs include base64-encoded page images (retrieval_info page_img rendered via PIL/matplotlib) and polar-plot figures — the notebook file is 552KB mostly from outputs; strip all outputs. Code cells depend on a `client` initialized in cell 8, so component sections are conceptually but not executably self-contained — prepend client-setup context or note the dependency. Markdown cells for each component (parser, reranker, GLM, LMUnit) are dense, self-contained explanations that embed well as standalone chunks.
